sentences.text: Dyslipidemia and L D L Oxidation.

Hyperlipidemia, especially elevated L D L cholesterol, can lead to atherosclerosis through lipoprotein deposition inside the vessel wall and the induction of oxidized L D L, a key player in the formation of atherosclerotic plaques.
These atherogenic markers have been shown to be modulated through the consumption of grapes and grape products.
A recent paper reported that grape oil has the capacity to preserve compounds that prevent oxidative stress, including ascorbic acid, tocopherols, and procyanidins.
A meta-analysis of randomized clinical trials utilizing grape products was conducted.
Twenty-four articles, including 618 subjects assigned to grape, grape juice, and grape extract and 587 subjects assigned to placebo, were evaluated.

The administration of grape products was associated mainly with reductions in plasma L D L-cholesterol and oxidized L D L. Millar and colleagues utilized grape powder in individuals with metabolic syndrome, who were allocated to consume either 60 grams per day of freeze-dried powder or a placebo for 4 weeks.
After a 3-week washout period, they were allocated to the alternate treatment.
Although no changes in high-density lipoprotein function were observed between groups, a significant decrease in plasma triglycerides was observed during the period of grape powder intake.
This was an important finding because individuals in this study had elevated plasma triglycerides, a known biomarker that increases the risk of heart disease.
Razavi and colleagues conducted a study on 52 mildly hyperlipidemic subjects who were given either red grape seed extract or a placebo for 8 weeks.

Subjects consuming red grape seed extract had significant decreases in total cholesterol, L D L cholesterol, and oxidized L D L, suggesting that red grape seed extract can protect against the risk of atherosclerosis and cardiovascular disease.

In the study done by Toscano and colleagues mentioned above, the group of runners who were consuming grape juice also presented decreases in L D L cholesterol and increases in H D L cholesterol, which results in a healthier L D L to H D L ratio, a key biomarker of heart disease risk.
A randomized triple-blind and placebo-controlled study was conducted with statin-treated patients, 75 individuals, undergoing primary prevention of cardiovascular disease.

The effects of consuming 350 milligrams per day in a capsule of either a resveratrol-enriched grape extract, a grape extract, or a placebo for 6 months and a double dose for the next 6 months were investigated.

After a 1-year follow-up, resveratrol-enriched grape extract led to a significant decrease in L D L cholesterol, apolipoprotein-B100, and oxidized L D L, whereas the non-H D L-C to ApoB ratio increased.

text: Dyslipidemia and L D L Oxidation. Hyperlipidemia, especially elevated L D L cholesterol, can lead to atherosclerosis through lipoprotein deposition inside the vessel wall and the induction of oxidized L D L, a key player in the formation of atherosclerotic plaques. These atherogenic markers have been shown to be modulated through the consumption of grapes and grape products. A recent paper reported that grape oil has the capacity to preserve compounds that prevent oxidative stress, including ascorbic acid, tocopherols, and procyanidins. A meta-analysis of randomized clinical trials utilizing grape products was conducted. Twenty-four articles, including 618 subjects assigned to grape, grape juice, and grape extract and 587 subjects assigned to placebo, were evaluated. The administration of grape products was associated mainly with reductions in plasma L D L-cholesterol and oxidized L D L. Millar and colleagues utilized grape powder in individuals with metabolic syndrome, who were allocated to consume either 60 grams per day of freeze-dried powder or a placebo for 4 weeks. After a 3-week washout period, they were allocated to the alternate treatment. Although no changes in high-density lipoprotein function were observed between groups, a significant decrease in plasma triglycerides was observed during the period of grape powder intake. This was an important finding because individuals in this study had elevated plasma triglycerides, a known biomarker that increases the risk of heart disease. Razavi and colleagues conducted a study on 52 mildly hyperlipidemic subjects who were given either red grape seed extract or a placebo for 8 weeks. Subjects consuming red grape seed extract had significant decreases in total cholesterol, L D L cholesterol, and oxidized L D L, suggesting that red grape seed extract can protect against the risk of atherosclerosis and cardiovascular disease. In the study done by Toscano and colleagues mentioned above, the group of runners who were consuming grape juice also presented decreases in L D L cholesterol and increases in H D L cholesterol, which results in a healthier L D L to H D L ratio, a key biomarker of heart disease risk. A randomized triple-blind and placebo-controlled study was conducted with statin-treated patients, 75 individuals, undergoing primary prevention of cardiovascular disease. The effects of consuming 350 milligrams per day in a capsule of either a resveratrol-enriched grape extract, a grape extract, or a placebo for 6 months and a double dose for the next 6 months were investigated. After a 1-year follow-up, resveratrol-enriched grape extract led to a significant decrease in L D L cholesterol, apolipoprotein-B100, and oxidized L D L, whereas the non-H D L-C to ApoB ratio increased.
